content.text: Agentic reasoning is the research frontier that studies how language-model agents interleave thinking, planning, and acting to solve problems that cannot be answered in a single forward pass. Rather than treating reasoning as a static chain of text produced once, it treats reasoning as an adaptive process that queries tools, revises intermediate conclusions, and decides for itself when to stop.

Agentic reasoning refers to a family of methods in which a model does not merely emit an answer but conducts a deliberate, multi-step reasoning process that is coupled to the environment. The canonical primitive is the
: the model produces a thought, chooses an action (a tool call or an answer), observes a result, and repeats. This turns reasoning from a monologue into a feedback-controlled search.

The research area sits at the intersection of chain-of-thought prompting, reinforcement learning, and tool use. What distinguishes it from ordinary chain-of-thought is that intermediate steps have consequences the model can observe, so errors can be detected and corrected mid-trajectory instead of only after a wrong final answer is produced.

: they require decomposing a goal, gathering evidence, and synthesising a result over many steps. A single-pass model that hallucinates one intermediate fact ruins the whole answer. Agentic reasoning matters because it makes each step verifiable and recoverable, which raises the ceiling on task difficulty an agent can reliably reach.

It also matters economically. Test-time compute — spending more inference steps on hard problems — has become a primary axis of capability improvement, rivalling scaling of pretraining. Understanding how to structure that extra compute as productive reasoning rather than wasted tokens is now a central research question for frontier labs.
— how eliciting intermediate steps improves multi-step accuracy.
— how a model emits structured calls and consumes their results.
— reward, policy, and the credit-assignment problem over trajectories.
— breadth-first, depth-first, and best-first search, which underpin tree- and graph-structured reasoning.

ReAct interleaves free-form reasoning traces with actions. The reasoning trace lets the model plan and track state in natural language; the action grounds that plan in real observations. Formally, at step
the policy maps the history to either a thought or an action:
is fed back into the context, the model conditions later reasoning on real evidence rather than on its own unverified guesses.

Tree-of-Thoughts and Graph-of-Thoughts generalise the linear chain into a search over partial solutions. The agent proposes several candidate next steps, scores them with a value estimate, and expands the most promising — trading extra compute for a lower chance of committing early to a dead end.

Reflexion-style methods add a critic pass: after a failed attempt the agent writes a natural-language post-mortem, stores it in memory, and conditions the next attempt on it. This converts a failure signal into an explicit lesson the policy can reuse without any weight update.

The newest paradigm bakes agentic reasoning into the weights. Models are trained with reinforcement learning on verifiable tasks (math, code, tool use) so that long internal reasoning traces emerge from optimisation rather than from prompt engineering. The expected return the training maximises is:
is a full reasoning-and-acting episode and
rewards correct final answers and, sometimes, well-formed intermediate steps.

attrs.alt: Agentic reasoning loop: observe, reason, act, verify, update

content.text: The loop above is the shared skeleton behind ReAct, Reflexion, and RL-trained reasoners. The differences lie in how each stage is implemented: whether verification is a separate critic or an internal check, whether the reasoning is a single chain or a search tree, and whether improvement happens in-context or in the weights.

A practical agentic reasoner keeps a running scratchpad, exposes a small set of tools, and enforces a stopping rule. The loop below shows the mechanics with an explicit thought/action/observation cycle.

The critical design choices are the step budget (a hard cap on the loop to bound cost and prevent infinite reflection) and the stopping condition (a parseable answer marker). Without both, agentic reasoners drift or loop indefinitely.

A reflection layer wraps the base loop: on failure, generate a critique and prepend it to the next attempt. This is the mechanism behind measurable gains on tasks where a first attempt fails but the error is diagnosable.

def solve_with_reflection(question, checker, attempts=3):
    memory = []  # accumulated natural-language lessons
    for i in range(attempts):
        hint = ("\nLessons from past attempts:\n" + "\n".join(memory)) if memory else ""
        answer = run(question + hint)
        if checker(answer):          # verifiable success signal
            return answer
        critique = run(
            f"Your answer was '{answer}' and it was wrong. "
            f"In one sentence, state the likely mistake."
        )
        memory.append(f"- {critique.strip()}")
    return answer
— training a verifier to score each reasoning step, giving denser feedback than a single final-answer reward.
— compressing long RL-trained reasoning traces into smaller models that reason cheaply.
— letting the model decide when a computation is better delegated to code than done in-head.
— evidence that models often cannot reliably fix their own errors without an external signal, motivating grounded verification.
— characterising how accuracy improves as a function of reasoning tokens or search width.

attrs.alt: Decision guide: match the reasoning strategy to the problem's shape

content.text: The strategies are not mutually exclusive — production systems layer them, wrapping an RL-trained reasoner in an outer verify-and-retry loop and reserving search for the sub-problems that branch. The unifying principle is to spend compute where it is verifiable and to stop as soon as the answer is confirmed.
— asking a model to grade its own answer without an external checker often just launders the original error.
— omitting a step budget lets reflection spiral, burning tokens with no progress.
— more reasoning tokens help only up to a point; past it, accuracy plateaus or degrades.
— dropping observations from context forces the model to re-derive facts and reintroduce errors.
Ground verification in an external signal — a unit test, a calculator, a retrieval hit — never in the model's own confidence alone.
Cap the reasoning budget and log per-step cost so runaway trajectories are visible.
Prefer search (tree/graph) only when the task genuinely branches; for linear tasks it wastes compute.
Store reflections as concise, reusable lessons rather than verbose transcripts.

Think of agentic reasoning as the difference between an exam taken with a sealed booklet and one taken open-book with a lab bench. A single-pass model writes down its answer from memory and hopes it is right. An agentic reasoner works at the bench: it tries something, watches what happens, notices when a result contradicts its plan, and adjusts. The intelligence is not only in the first guess but in the loop of guess, test, and revise.
Agentic reasoning earns its extra cost only on problems where a single pass is unreliable. Use it, and avoid it, according to these criteria:
the task decomposes into verifiable sub-steps — multi-hop research, math, code, or anything with tools that can check intermediate results.
a first attempt can be graded, so reflection has a real signal to learn from.
the task is a simple lookup or single computation a base model handles in one pass — the loop only adds latency and cost.
there is no external verifier and the model would only grade itself, which rarely improves accuracy.
(tree/graph) only when the problem genuinely branches into distinct candidate paths worth comparing.

Competition-math and coding assistants are the clearest production use. A system solving a hard problem samples many reasoning trajectories, filters them through an executable checker (unit tests for code, a symbolic checker for math), and returns only a verified solution. This pattern — generate broadly, verify strictly — is how test-time compute converts into reliable answers.
Accuracy typically rises with reasoning compute, but with diminishing returns that follow an approximately logarithmic curve in the number of samples or reasoning tokens:

while accuracy grows logarithmically, there is a compute budget past which extra reasoning is not worth its price. Parallel sampling scales throughput but not per-trajectory latency; sequential reflection improves quality but serialises the work. Production systems tune the budget per task difficulty rather than applying a fixed depth everywhere.
— LangGraph, DSPy, and similar libraries structure reason–act loops and retries.
— unit-test runners, symbolic math checkers, and process reward models supply the external grading signal.
— LangSmith and OpenTelemetry-based tracers log every step so runaway trajectories are debuggable.
— RL-trained models expose long internal reasoning as a first-class capability.

Longer autonomous loops widen the attack and failure surface. Tool outputs can carry injected instructions, so treat every observation as untrusted input rather than as a trusted continuation of the model's own reasoning.
— sanitise and clearly delimit tool results; never let a retrieved document silently redirect the plan.
— hard-cap steps, tokens, and wall-clock time so a stuck loop cannot run up unbounded cost.
— log seeds and full trajectories so failures are reproducible and auditable.
agentic reasoning is just a longer prompt.
It is a closed loop with the environment; the defining feature is that actions produce observations that reshape later reasoning.
more reasoning tokens always improve accuracy.
Returns diminish and can reverse; the useful quantity is productive, verifiable reasoning, not raw length.
a model can always fix its own mistakes if asked to reflect.
Reliable self-correction usually needs an external verification signal; ungrounded reflection frequently reinforces the original error.
Agentic reasoning couples thinking to acting in a loop, making intermediate steps observable and correctable.
The reason–act loop (ReAct) is the shared primitive; search and reflection are elaborations of it.
RL-trained reasoning models internalise long reasoning traces, making test-time compute a first-class scaling axis.
Verification must be grounded externally; self-grading alone is unreliable.
Always bound the reasoning budget — cost and correctness both depend on knowing when to stop.
